blocks.content: Two houses down the lane, in a rental that was slightly smaller and faced slightly less directly toward the water, Julian Cross was standing on the back porch with a cup of coffee and the expression of a man who had not yet decided whether he was going to allow this vacation to happen to him.

He was 28. Lean in the way of someone who forgot to eat when he was working, which was most of the time. He had the kind of face that people found difficult to read — not cold, exactly, but precise, like every expression had been considered before being deployed. His eyes were the sort that moved differently from everyone else's in a room. Not faster. Just — differently. Taking different inventory.

He heard the cars before he saw them. Two, arriving within a minute of each other, gravel under tires, doors opening, voices carrying across the dunes in the particular pitch of people glad to have arrived somewhere.

He watched the group unload from a distance without meaning to. It was not surveillance. It was simply that Julian Cross, constitutionally, noticed things. It cost him nothing. It happened the way breathing happened — automatically, below the level of decision.
Six people. Three men, three women. The energy of a group that knew each other well — the shorthand, the casual physical ease, the way they moved around each other without having to think about it.
Mara Voss was at the kitchen table with her laptop, which she had promised Julian she would not bring and had brought anyway. She looked up when he came in.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: We don't need to do that.

blocks.speaker: Mara
blocks.content: We're on vacation, Julian. Vacation means talking to people you don't know.

blocks.speaker: Mara
blocks.content: It sounds like being a human being.
Julian refilled his coffee and sat across from her and did not agree to anything.
